query: evidence on CA72-4 levels in ovarian endometriomas vs epithelial ovarian cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recurrent Ovarian Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: The Desktop 3 trial, which compares surgery followed by chemotherapy versus chemotherapy only in recurrent platinum-sensitive ovarian cancer, is currently ongoing, and its results are eagerly awaited. Preliminary results showed improvement in progression-free survival and longer intervals to the start of subsequent chemotherapy in favor of surgery followed by chemotherapy. Additionally, 2 other trials, Surgery for Ovarian Cancer Recurrence (SOCceR) and Surgery or Chemotherapy in Recurrent Ovarian Cancer (SOC 1), are comparing surgery and chemotherapy with surgery alone in such groups of patients; the results are still awaited. However, current evidence has not shown longer overall survival with second-degree surgical cytoreduction in patients with platinum-sensitive recurrent epithelial ovarian cancer diagnosed surgery. [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Krukenberg Tumor -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Primary ovarian cancer patients are usually diagnosed at 65 years of age versus younger than 50 years of age for Krukenberg patients. [26] The CA-125 tumor marker is usually higher in primary ovarian cancer than in Krukenberg tumors. Median CA-125 levels are 652 u/mL for the former and 43 u/mL for the latter. Both CEA and cancer antigen 19-9 (CA19-9) are, on average, higher in Krukenberg tumors than in primary ovarian malignancies: 5.4 ng/mL (CEA) and 65.9 u/mL (CA19-9) vs 1.4 ng/mL (CEA) and 18.0 u/mL (CA19-9) respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies and Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor markers are usually measured in conjunction with imaging studies of patients with suspected malignancy. Human gonadotropin, alpha-fetoprotein, and carcinoembryonic antigen tumor markers should be obtained to help exclude germ-cell and gastrointestinal malignancies. [22] Cancer antigen 125 (CA-125) is a glycoprotein produced by Müllerian epithelium that is detectable by serum laboratory studies and the most recommended biomarker to evaluate suspected ovarian cancer. CA-125 levels are increased in most advanced epithelial ovarian cancer cases but are elevated only in 50% of early-stage disease cases; therefore, the sensitivity of this biomarker is limited. The specificity and positive predictive value are higher in postmenopausal women than in premenopausal women. A CA-125 level greater than 35 U/mL in a woman who is postmenopausal indicates a high malignancy risk. However, CA-125 is not specific to epithelial ovarian cancers as it can also be elevated in patients with other conditions, including pregnancy, nonovarian malignancies, and inflammatory pathologies (eg, acute pelvic inflammatory disease, adenomyosis, and endometriosis). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for ovarian cancer includes: Colon cancer Embryologic remnants Gastric adenocarcinoma Metastatic gastrointestinal carcinoma Ovarian torsion Peritoneal cyst Retroperitoneal mass Uterine fibroids Endometriosis Papillary adenocarcinoma Serous adenocarcinomas Undifferentiated adenocarcinomas Small-cell adenocarcinomas Brenner tumors

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies and Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Various diagnostic formulas using tumor markers have been proposed to calculate the risk of epithelial ovarian cancer. CA-125 levels are used to calculate the malignancy index (RMI) risk. The RMI is a multiple of CA-125, transvaginal ultrasound parameters, and menopausal status to determine the risk of an adnexal mass being an ovarian malignancy. [28] An RMI, particularly in postmenopausal women, higher than 200 is associated with a high risk of malignancy, with a specificity of more than 96%. [28] The risk of ovarian malignancy algorithm (ROMA) utilizes a mathematical formula that incorporates HE4 and CA-125 levels adjusted for premenopausal and postmenopausal status to determine the risk of malignancy. [31] However, these formulas are less effective in predicting the malignancy of an adnexal mass than risk stratification systems based on imaging studies. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Omental Tumors -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: An observational cross-sectional study compared persons with metastatic ovarian cancer of the same stage and similar characteristics, differentiated by the presence or absence of omental metastases. Of the 56 persons, 36 had omental metastases, and 20 had no omental metastases. All underwent debulking surgery. The patients had no significant difference in tumor size, CA-125 levels, or ascites. Nearly all received platinum-based chemotherapy. The overall survival rate in those with omental metastases was 43.4% compared with 93.8% in those without omental metastases. In addition, progression-free and post-recurrence survival was shorter, and the recurrence rate was higher (72.2% vs 40.0%) in the omental metastases group. The authors concluded that omental metastases are an independent risk factor for survival in patients with stage III-IV ovarian cancer. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate between symptoms of ovarian cancer and those of other gynecological conditions to facilitate early diagnosis and intervention. Screen patients for ovarian cancer using appropriate methods, such as pelvic examinations, transvaginal ultrasound, and tumor marker testing. Implement evidence-based diagnostic and treatment protocols for ovarian cancer, including biopsy, imaging studies, and multimodal therapy. Apply interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and outcomes in patients with ovarian cancer. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies and Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Other investigated biomarkers include folate receptor alpha, CA72-4, transthyretin, CA15-3, and glycodelin. CA-125 remains the most beneficial and commonly used tumor marker for evaluating ovarian cancer risk. However, these other tumor markers may have more utility when combined with CA-125. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies and Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Human epididymis protein 4 (HE4) is a peptide protease inhibitor found in epididymal epithelium. Though not typically found in ovarian epithelium, HE4 is detected by serum laboratory studies in ovarian cancer tissue with a specificity of 96%. This biomarker is not diagnostic for ovarian cancer, though, as HE4 is also elevated in other malignancies (eg, endometrial cancer and lung adenocarcinomas). HE4 has a higher sensitivity than CA-125 in early-stage ovarian cancers and a higher specificity in late-stage disease. However, CA-125 has a higher sensitivity in late-stage ovarian cancers than HE4. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriomas carry a small risk of developing a malignancy, specifically epithelial ovarian cancers. However, that risk appears low. A meta-analysis of 13 studies and approximately 9000 women with epithelial ovarian cancers showed that a self-reported history of endometriosis was three times as high. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Surveillance
retrieved_docs.content: Following primary ovarian cancer treatment, continued monitoring for any evidence of recurrence is necessary, though the precise duration and frequency have not been universally established. The NCCN guidelines recommend follow-up visits with a pelvic exam every 2 to 4 months for 2 years, then every 3 to 6 months for 3 years. After the first 5 years, follow-up can be performed annually. Furthermore, a CA-125 level should be assessed in patients with elevated levels before treatment. A complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic profile, and imaging should be performed when clinically indicated. [64] Imaging modalities include chest, abdominal, and pelvic MRI, computed tomography (CT), or positron emission tomography (PET)/CT scan. NCCN guidelines also recommend that a genetic testing referral be offered if it has not already been done. However, some experts recommend routinely imaging and assessing a CA-125 level in patients with stage II to IV ovarian cancers every 3 months the first year, every 4 months the second year, every 6 months in years 3 to 5, and then every year thereafter, instead of just as clinically indicated. Monitoring bone mineral density in patients receiving an aromatase inhibitor is also recommended. [64] [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer Antigen 125 -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Study results have shown that the test exhibits a higher sensitivity and specificity in postmenopausal women compared to premenopausal women. [32] [33] The low sensitivity is due in part to the fact that CA125 is elevated only in half of the patients with early-stage epithelial ovarian cancer and rarely elevated in patients with mucinous carcinomas of the ovary. The low specificity, especially among premenopausal women, is due to the elevation caused by benign conditions such as fibroids, endometriosis, and pregnancy. [4] However, the test is relatively sensitive and specific among patients who have completed treatment for ovarian cancer. Patients who appear to be clinically free of the tumor with elevations in CA125 (>35 U/mL) almost always have a tumor in second-look surgery. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies and Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Serum laboratory testing should typically include a complete blood count and metabolic profile. [7] The American Society of Clinical Oncology guidelines recommend offering genetic testing for BRCA1 and BRCA2 to all women with epithelial ovarian cancer, and in patients with clear cell, endometrioid, or mucinous ovarian cancer subtypes, MMRd molecular testing should be offered . [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recurrent Ovarian Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Large phase 3 trials have also resulted in the approval of bevacizumab, which was studied in combination with chemotherapy for the treatment of recurrent ovarian cancer as well as for maintenance therapy (GOG-218, or OCEANS and AURELIA trials). [57] The studies have shown an objective improvement in progression-free survival. However, they failed to prove a benefit in overall survival. Nevertheless, anti-angiogenic agents have shown activity in these platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancers, though further studies are needed to define their benefits clearly. Evidence shows the use of aromatase inhibitors like letrozole for the treatment of recurrent low-grade serous and endometrioid epithelial ovarian cancer based on large retrospective cohort studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Etiology -- Ovarian Cancer Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Factors that increase ovulation over a lifetime (eg, nulliparity, early menarche, or late menopause) are associated with increased ovarian cancer risk. However, the exact etiologic mechanism is not known. [13] Additionally, inflammatory conditions (eg, endometriosis and obesity) are thought to lead to the development of ovarian cancer secondary to oxidative stress and deoxyribonucleic acid damage. [14] [15] A significant risk factor for ovarian cancer is a positive personal or family history of breast or ovarian cancer. Germline mutations in BRCA1 or BRCA2 genes are a prevalent underlying cause of malignancy predisposition in individuals. Other hereditary cancer syndromes that also increase the risk for ovarian cancer are associated with other gene mutations, including mismatch repair genes in Lynch syndrome, tumor protein p53 ( TP53) in Li-Fraumeni syndrome, STK11 in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, CHEK2 , RAD51 , BRIP1 , and PALB2 . [16] Recent data suggest that high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) may originate in the fallopian tube based on observed changes in the distal epithelium in fallopian serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma, an HGSC precursor. [17] [18] Studies have not found proposed ovarian cancer screening strategies to be effective; still, many organizations have agreed that offering various screening methods to these high-risk patients is appropriate. [7] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer Antigen 125 -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: In the original CA125 IRMA test, the cut-off point for the upper limit of normal was arbitrarily set at 35 U/mL. This threshold was chosen because only 1% of the healthy population had CA125 levels greater than 35 U/mL. However, this was reduced to 0.2% of the healthy population when the cut-off point for the upper limit was raised to 65 U/mL. [7] The second-generation CA125 has shown to be more clinically reliable with greater precision in values less than 35 U/mL, leading to higher values compared to the original CA125 test. [22] A meta-analysis comparing various studies using a CA125 threshold of greater than 35 U/mL for preoperative identification of an adnexal mass suspicious for ovarian cancer revealed an overall sensitivity and specificity of 78.7% and 77.9%, respectively. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2800
retrieved_docs.content: إزالة الرحم (uterus removal) is classified within the broader category of Female Reproductive System Procedures, indicating that it is a specific type of gynecologic surgery. Clinical practice frequently shows that إزالة الرحم co‑occurs with استئصال البوق والمبيض (removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries), meaning that these two procedures are often performed together. The combined approach reflects common therapeutic strategies for conditions such as gynecologic malignancies, severe endometriosis, or prophylactic risk reduction, where simultaneous removal of the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries may be indicated to achieve optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not considered a finding associated with ovarian cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Ovarian cancer, a malignant neoplasm originating in the ovary, is associated with the concept of metastases to the ovary (نقائل إلى المبيض), indicating that secondary tumor spread can involve the ovarian tissue itself. Recognizing that ovarian cancer may include metastatic lesions to the ovary is important for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and the formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2845
retrieved_docs.content: The female reproductive system encompasses surgical interventions such as abdominal hysterectomy, a procedure in which the uterus is removed through an abdominal incision. Abdominal hysterectomy frequently co‑occurs with salpingo‑oophorectomy, the removal of the fallopian tube and ovary, indicating that these gynecologic surgeries are often performed together. This combined approach is commonly employed in the management of conditions affecting the uterus and adnexal structures, such as malignant or benign tumors, severe endometriosis, or other complex pelvic diseases, and informs surgical planning and patient counseling in women’s health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2590
retrieved_docs.content: In the context of ovarian cancer, tumor cells that are benign are not considered a disease‑related finding, indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of cancer of ovary. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are classified as abnormal cells of cancer of ovary, reflecting a potential pathological significance that may require further evaluation. This distinction emphasizes the importance of accurately characterizing cellular morphology when assessing ovarian pathology and guiding appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2887
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method includes the procedure استئصال قناة فالوب (salpingectomy) as a recognized value, indicating that salpingectomy can be used as a method for obtaining biospecimens. In clinical practice, the surgical removal of ovarian cysts, إزالة تكيسات المبيض, frequently co‑occurs with استئصال قناة فالوب, suggesting that these two gynecologic surgeries are often performed together when collecting tissue samples or addressing concurrent pelvic pathology. This relationship highlights the interconnected nature of gynecologic operative techniques and their combined relevance for both diagnostic specimen acquisition and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding indicative of cancer of ovary, reflecting the presence of malignant cells infiltrating ovarian tissue in patients with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882
retrieved_docs.content: Partial salpingo‑oophorectomy is frequently performed together with partial hysterectomy (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا). Likewise, partial removal of the ovary (إزالة المبيض جزئيًا) co‑occurs with partial hysterectomy, and bilateral salpingo‑oophorectomy (استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي) is also commonly combined with partial hysterectomy. The repeated co‑occurrence of these procedures indicates that surgeons often address uterine and adnexal pathology in a single operative session, which can be advantageous for conditions such as extensive fibroids, endometriosis, or gynecologic malignancies by providing comprehensive treatment and reducing the need for multiple separate surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2164
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of ovary is recognized as a distinct malignant disease whose abnormal cellular component is described as “Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant,” indicating that the neoplastic cells in ovarian cancer may initially be difficult to classify but are ultimately considered abnormal cells of the disease. In the diagnostic work‑up, cancer of ovary must be differentiated from سرطان القولون (colon cancer), as both can present with overlapping pelvic or abdominal findings and therefore appear on each other’s differential diagnosis lists. Additionally, cancer of ovary can give rise to or be associated with نقائل إلى المبيض, which denotes metastatic lesions that involve the ovary. Understanding that ovarian cancer may be confused with colon cancer, that its tumor cells are abnormal by nature, and that it can produce ovarian metastases is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells that cannot be definitively classified as benign or malignant are considered abnormal cells in the context of ovarian cancer, indicating that such indeterminate tumor cells represent the pathological cellular component of this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2815
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad category encompassing various gynecologic conditions. It includes specific disorders such as endometriosis, which is classified as a type of uterine disorder (اضطراب رحمي). The category also relates to the parent condition اضطراب رحمي itself and to ovarian cyst (كيسة مبيضية). Recognizing that endometriosis falls under the umbrella of uterine disorders within the larger Female Reproductive System Disorder framework helps clinicians assess and manage these interconnected conditions more comprehensively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1901
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding associated with cancer of ovary, indicating that the presence of malignant cells infiltrating tissue is a diagnostic feature of ovarian malignancy. Cancer of ovary also encompasses the concept of نقائل إلى المبيض, which refers to metastatic lesions that spread to the ovary, highlighting that ovarian cancer can involve secondary tumor deposits within the ovarian tissue. Together, these relationships emphasize that malignant cellular infiltration serves as a key histologic indicator of ovarian cancer and that ovarian cancer may present with metastatic involvement of the ovary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2092
retrieved_docs.content: of both ovaries and the uterus (استئصال المبيضين والرحم), reflecting the spectrum of surgical options from organ‑preserving to radical excision. Overall, these interrelated procedures illustrate a structured taxonomy of uterine, cervical, and ovarian surgeries, their co‑occurrence patterns, and their inclusion in a unified classification of malignant‑disease‑related therapeutic and diagnostic interventions, which is essential for accurate coding, clinical decision‑making, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding of cancer of the ovary, indicating the presence of abnormal tissue within the ovarian region. Similarly, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component of cancer of the ovary, representing the malignant cells that drive the disease process. Together, these pathological entities underscore the cellular abnormalities that characterize ovarian cancer and are essential for its diagnosis and understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2502
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of ovary is defined by the presence of tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, which are recognized as abnormal cells of the disease. In addition, cancer of ovary includes the related concept “نقائل إلى المبيض,” indicating metastatic involvement of the ovary. This reflects that ovarian cancer comprises ambiguous tumor cells and may be associated with metastatic spread to the ovary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1091
retrieved_docs.content: encompasses organ‑specific biopsies (ovarian and uterine), the uterine biopsy further subdivides into endometrial biopsy, and all are situated within the broader histopathology framework overseen by obstetrics‑gynecology specialists, who also perform related procedures such as salpingo‑oophorectomy and endometrial sampling. This integrated structure aids clinicians in understanding procedural categorization, appropriate specialist involvement, and the interconnections among related diagnostic and therapeutic interventions.
